hook: Tiger Balm fortune turned into a gory Confucian theme park
facts: Built 1937 by Tiger Balm magnate Aw Boon Haw for his brother Aw Boon Par
Holds over 1,000 statues and 150 dioramas of Chinese myth and folklore
Ten Courts of Hell depicts graphic punishments for earthly sins
Hell's Museum, on death and afterlife, opened on the grounds in 2021
legend: Generations of Singaporean children recall being marched past the disembowelling demons as a morality lesson
w: A balm baron spent his menthol fortune on 1,000 lurid statues so the public could grasp Chinese morality the hard way. The Ten Courts of Hell still show sinners sawn in half, boiled and disembowelled by grinning demons. Free, gloriously tasteless, and quietly traumatising to every local child marched through it.

hook: Vast forgotten Chinese necropolis half-eaten by a highway
facts: Opened 1922 as a Chinese municipal burial ground, named after shipowner George Henry Brown
Believed the largest Chinese cemetery outside China, with around 100,000 graves
Closed to burials in 1973 after running out of plots in 1944
Around 3,700 graves were exhumed from 2012 to build an eight-lane road
legend: Sikh guardian statues and tiger carvings were placed to ward spirits from the grandest tombs
w: A hundred thousand pioneers lie under this hill, the largest Chinese cemetery outside China, slowly swallowed by jungle and forgetting. When the state ran a highway through it in 2012, volunteers raced to record names off mossy tombstones before the diggers came. Tigers and Sikh sentinels still guard graves nobody visits.

hook: Holy man's tomb that refused to be buried anywhere else
facts: Habib Noh, a revered Hadhrami saint, was buried atop Mount Palmer in 1866 by his own request
A staircase of 49 steps climbs to the maqam above Palmer Road
Masjid Haji Muhammad Salleh below dates to 1903
Reopened January 2026 after extensive renovation
legend: They say his coffin became unliftable until pallbearers agreed to bury him on the hill
A landowner who demanded a fee for the plot was said to drop dead within three days
w: When the saint died in 1866 they meant to bury him at Bidadari, but legend says his coffin grew too heavy to move until the bearers relented and carried him up Mount Palmer. Forty-nine steps climb to his shrine, marooned now among Shenton Way towers. Pilgrims still climb. A holy man who chose his own hill.

hook: Art Deco car plant where Britain surrendered Singapore
facts: Ford's first Southeast Asian assembly plant, opened October 1941
Here Lt-Gen Percival surrendered to Yamashita on 15 February 1942
The surrender was signed in the factory boardroom around 6pm
Gazetted a National Monument in 2006; now a WWII archive and gallery
w: Ford had been bolting together cars at this streamlined Deco plant for barely four months when, on 15 February 1942, General Percival walked into its boardroom and handed Singapore to the Japanese. The largest British capitulation in history, signed beside an assembly line. The preserved boardroom table sits where the surrender was inked.

hook: Forgotten coastal gun tunnels found by accident in 2001
facts: Part of Fort Pasir Panjang, a coastal artillery fort completed 1878
Upgraded to a six-gun battery with underground stores and tunnels by 1892
Two 6-inch guns here fired inland at advancing Japanese in February 1942
At least nine tunnels lay lost under vegetation until rediscovered in 2001
w: Britain dug guns and ammunition tunnels into this seaside ridge to guard Keppel Harbour, then watched the Japanese arrive overland instead. The cannon turned inland and fired anyway before the fall. After the war the warren of nine tunnels simply vanished under creeper until rangers stumbled into them in 2001. War sealed in a hillside.

hook: Brothel keeper's gift to destitute Japanese sex workers
facts: Founded 1891 when brothel owner Tagajiro Fukaki donated rubber plantation land
Largest Japanese cemetery in Southeast Asia, with around 910 tombstones
Nearly half the graves belong to karayuki-san, impoverished Japanese prostitutes
Holds a memorial to Field Marshal Terauchi, who led Japan's southern conquest
w: A brothel keeper gave over his rubber land in 1891 so the karayuki-san, Japanese girls sold into Singapore's red-light streets, would not die in unmarked graves. Half the 910 stones here mark those forgotten women, lying a few rows from a field marshal who led the conquest of the south. Empire and its outcasts under one canopy.

hook: Singapore's only mainland hot spring, found in a pineapple field
facts: Hot water found 1909 by merchant Seah Eng Keong on his pineapple estate
Spring water holds a constant 70 degrees Celsius
Once bottled commercially by Fraser and Neave as Seletaris
Reopened as a public park in January 2020
w: A merchant prodding his pineapple estate in 1909 hit water hot enough to boil an egg, the only natural hot spring on Singapore's mainland. Locals still bring eggs in mesh bags to cook in the 70-degree flow, then soak their feet at the cascade. F and N once bottled it as Seletaris. A volcanic quirk beside an army camp.

hook: Leaning minaret of Singapore, built by a robbed businesswoman
facts: Completed 1846, the first local mosque named after a woman
Hajjah Fatimah built it after thieves attacked her former home on the site
Its minaret tilts about six degrees on sandy foundations
Gazetted a National Monument in 1973
w: A wealthy Malay businesswoman, twice robbed and once nearly killed in her home, razed the house and raised a mosque on the spot in 1846, the first here named for a woman. Its minaret, on shifting sand, leans a stubborn six degrees, earning it the leaning tower of Singapore. Gratitude rendered in tilting brick.

hook: Colonial police HQ with 927 rainbow-painted windows
facts: Built 1934 as a police station and barracks, once Singapore's largest government building
Holds 927 windows, painted in rainbow colours since a 1999 restoration
Reused as offices for the arts and information ministry from 2000
Gazetted a National Monument in 1998
w: In 1934 this six-storey block held Singapore's biggest police station and a barracks, a stern grey slab of colonial order. Restorers in 1999 did the unthinkable and painted all 927 shuttered windows in the full spectrum, turning a monument to discipline into a wall of rainbow. Bureaucracy never looked so cheerful.

n: Sun Yat Sen Nanyang Memorial Hall
hook: Suburban villa that plotted China's 1911 revolution
facts: Colonial villa known as Wan Qing Yuan, off Ah Hood Road in Balestier
Served as the Tongmenghui's Southeast Asian base in the early 1900s
Sun Yat Sen visited Singapore nine times between 1900 and 1911
Now a museum on the overseas Chinese role in the Xinhai Revolution
w: Behind this genteel Balestier villa, revolutionaries planned the overthrow of China's last dynasty. Sun Yat Sen made it his Southeast Asian headquarters, slipping into Singapore nine times to raise funds and recruit before the 1911 revolution. A quiet two-storey house where an emperor's fate was quietly decided overseas.

hook: Five floors of vintage toys from one obsessive collector
facts: Opened 2007 on Seah Street, built around Chang Yang Fa's private hoard
Holds over 50,000 toys and collectibles from the 1800s to 1980s
MINT stands for Moment of Imagination and Nostalgia with Toys
Pieces include rare tin robots, antique teddy bears and early character toys
w: One man spent thirty years amassing 50,000 toys, then built a glass tower to house them. Tin robots, prewar teddy bears, Dan Dare ray guns and Batman tat from a century of childhood crowd five hushed floors off Seah Street. Less a museum than one collector's beautifully lit obsession, dignified at last behind glass.

hook: Only surviving civilian WWII shelter, hidden in a flat block
facts: Built into a 1930s SIT flat block at 78 Guan Chuan Street
Singapore's only purpose-built civilian air raid shelter from the era
Used by residents during Japanese bombing raids in WWII
Opened to public guided tours in 2018
w: Tucked into the ground floor of a streamlined 1930s flat block sits the country's only purpose-built civilian air raid shelter, where Tiong Bahru residents huddled through Japanese bombing. Sealed and near-forgotten for decades behind an ordinary stairwell, it opened to small guided tours only in 2018. War in the basement of home.
